prescribing_info.title: ELIQUIS (apixaban) Prescribing Information
prescribing_info.content:
ELIQUIS (APIXABAN) TABLETS - FULL PRESCRIBING INFORMATION

MANUFACTURER: Bristol-Myers Squibb/Pfizer

BLACK BOX WARNINGS:
1. PREMATURE DISCONTINUATION: Increased risk of thrombotic events. If anticoagulation must be discontinued for reason other than pathological bleeding or completion of therapy, consider coverage with another anticoagulant.

2. SPINAL/EPIDURAL HEMATOMA: Epidural or spinal hematomas may occur in patients anticoagulated with ELIQUIS who receive neuraxial anesthesia or spinal puncture. May result in long-term or permanent paralysis.

MECHANISM: Direct Factor Xa inhibitor

INDICATIONS:
- Stroke and systemic embolism risk reduction in nonvalvular atrial fibrillation
- DVT/PE treatment
- DVT/PE prophylaxis after hip or knee replacement
- Reduction in risk of recurrent DVT/PE

DOSAGE - ATRIAL FIBRILLATION:
- Standard dose: 5 mg twice daily
- Reduced dose: 2.5 mg twice daily

Dose Reduction Criteria (need 2 or more):
- Age ≥80 years
- Body weight ≤60 kg
- Serum creatinine ≥1.5 mg/dL

DOSAGE - DVT/PE TREATMENT:
- Initial: 10 mg twice daily for 7 days
- Then: 5 mg twice daily
- After 6 months (recurrence prevention): 2.5 mg twice daily

DOSAGE - DVT PROPHYLAXIS:
- Hip replacement: 2.5 mg twice daily for 35 days
- Knee replacement: 2.5 mg twice daily for 12 days
- Start 12-24 hours post-surgery

RENAL DOSING:
- eGFR ≥25: No adjustment beyond standard criteria
- eGFR 15-24: Limited data, use with caution
- eGFR <15 or dialysis: Not recommended
- ESRD on dialysis with AF: 5 mg twice daily (no dose reduction criteria apply)

MAXIMUM DOSES:
- Standard: 5 mg twice daily
- Never exceed 10 mg per day for maintenance
- DVT/PE treatment first 7 days: 10 mg twice daily is maximum

CONTRAINDICATIONS:
- Active pathological bleeding
- Severe hypersensitivity to apixaban

WARNINGS:
- Bleeding risk: GI bleeding, intracranial hemorrhage
- Prosthetic heart valves: Not studied, avoid use
- Triple positive antiphospholipid syndrome: Avoid use

DRUG INTERACTIONS:
- Strong dual CYP3A4 and P-gp inhibitors (ketoconazole, ritonavir): Decrease dose by 50% OR avoid if already on reduced dose
- Strong dual CYP3A4 and P-gp inducers (rifampin, phenytoin): Avoid concomitant use

SURGICAL INTERRUPTION:
- Low bleeding risk: Skip 24 hours before procedure
- Moderate/high bleeding risk: Skip 48 hours before
- Resume 24-48 hours after hemostasis achieved

REVERSAL:
- Andexanet alfa (Andexxa): FDA-approved reversal agent
- Activated charcoal if ingestion within 2-6 hours
- 4-Factor PCC may be considered

ADVERSE REACTIONS:
- Bleeding (major 2.1%)
- Anemia
- Nausea
- Bruising

MONITORING:
- No routine monitoring required
- Anti-Xa levels if needed (calibrated for apixaban)
- CBC, renal function periodically

HOW SUPPLIED:
- 2.5 mg tablets
- 5 mg tablets

SOURCE PROVENANCE:
This document summarizes key information from the FDA-approved prescribing information.
FDA Application: NDA 202155
Label Revision: 01/2024
For complete prescribing information, refer to the official FDA label at: https://dailymed.nlm.nih.gov/dailymed/drugInfo.cfm?setid=0c29f52e-6c5b-4b95-a7a9-c8d47c9e7f85

prescribing_info.title: XARELTO (rivaroxaban) Prescribing Information
prescribing_info.content:
XARELTO (RIVAROXABAN) TABLETS - FULL PRESCRIBING INFORMATION

MANUFACTURER: Janssen Pharmaceuticals

BLACK BOX WARNINGS:
1. Premature discontinuation increases stroke risk
2. Spinal/epidural hematoma risk with neuraxial anesthesia

MECHANISM: Direct Factor Xa inhibitor

INDICATIONS:
- Nonvalvular atrial fibrillation stroke prevention
- DVT/PE treatment and prevention
- Post-hip/knee replacement DVT prophylaxis
- Reduction of major cardiovascular events with CAD/PAD

DOSAGE - ATRIAL FIBRILLATION:
- CrCl >50 mL/min: 20 mg once daily with evening meal
- CrCl 15-50 mL/min: 15 mg once daily with evening meal
- CrCl <15 mL/min: Avoid use

DOSAGE - DVT/PE TREATMENT:
- Initial 21 days: 15 mg twice daily with food
- After 21 days: 20 mg once daily with food
- Continued treatment (after 6 months): 10 mg once daily with or without food

DOSAGE - DVT PROPHYLAXIS:
- Hip replacement: 10 mg once daily x35 days
- Knee replacement: 10 mg once daily x12 days
- Start 6-10 hours post-surgery after hemostasis

DOSAGE - CAD/PAD:
- 2.5 mg twice daily plus aspirin 75-100 mg daily

MAXIMUM DOSES:
- AF: 20 mg once daily
- DVT/PE treatment: 15 mg twice daily (first 21 days)
- Do not exceed labeled maximum doses

CRITICAL: TAKE WITH FOOD
- 15 mg and 20 mg doses MUST be taken with food
- 10 mg can be taken with or without food
- Food increases bioavailability

RENAL DOSING:
- CrCl ≥50: Full dose
- CrCl 15-49 (AF): 15 mg daily
- CrCl <15: Avoid use
- Dialysis: Not recommended

HEPATIC:
- Child-Pugh B/C: Avoid use
- Moderate hepatic impairment with coagulopathy: Avoid

CONTRAINDICATIONS:
- Active pathological bleeding
- Severe hypersensitivity

DRUG INTERACTIONS:
- Strong CYP3A4 and P-gp inhibitors: Avoid in CrCl 15-80
- Strong CYP3A4 inducers: Avoid concomitant use
- Combined P-gp and CYP3A4 inhibitors/inducers: Use caution

SURGICAL INTERRUPTION:
- Discontinue at least 24 hours before surgery
- Higher bleeding risk procedures: 48 hours or longer
- Bridging anticoagulation generally not required

REVERSAL:
- Andexanet alfa (Andexxa): FDA-approved
- Activated charcoal within 8 hours
- 4-Factor PCC may partially reverse

MONITORING:
- No routine monitoring required
- Anti-Xa activity (rivaroxaban-calibrated) if needed

HOW SUPPLIED:
- 2.5 mg, 10 mg, 15 mg, 20 mg tablets
- Starter pack for DVT/PE (51 x 15mg tablets)

SOURCE PROVENANCE:
This document summarizes key information from the FDA-approved prescribing information.
FDA Application: NDA 202439
Label Revision: 11/2023
For complete prescribing information, refer to the official FDA label at: https://dailymed.nlm.nih.gov/dailymed/drugInfo.cfm?setid=10db92f9-2300-4a80-836b-673e1ae91610

prescribing_info.title: ENTRESTO (sacubitril/valsartan) Prescribing Information
prescribing_info.content:
ENTRESTO (SACUBITRIL/VALSARTAN) - FULL PRESCRIBING INFORMATION

MANUFACTURER: Novartis

BLACK BOX WARNING:
FETAL TOXICITY - Drugs that act directly on the renin-angiotensin system can cause injury and death to the developing fetus. Discontinue as soon as pregnancy is detected.

MECHANISM: Angiotensin receptor-neprilysin inhibitor (ARNI)
- Sacubitril: Neprilysin inhibitor (increases natriuretic peptides)
- Valsartan: ARB (blocks angiotensin II)

INDICATIONS:
- Reduce risk of cardiovascular death and hospitalization for heart failure in patients with chronic HF (NYHA II-IV) with reduced ejection fraction
- HFpEF: To reduce hospitalization

DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION:

Starting Dose:
- Not currently on ACE-I or ARB OR low-dose ACE-I/ARB equivalent:
  - Start: 24/26 mg (sacubitril/valsartan) twice daily
- Currently on moderate-high dose ACE-I/ARB:
  - Start: 49/51 mg twice daily

Titration:
- Double dose every 2-4 weeks as tolerated
- Target dose: 97/103 mg twice daily

Dose Levels:
- 24/26 mg = 24 mg sacubitril / 26 mg valsartan
- 49/51 mg = 49 mg sacubitril / 51 mg valsartan  
- 97/103 mg = 97 mg sacubitril / 103 mg valsartan

MAXIMUM DOSE:
- 97/103 mg twice daily (194/206 mg total daily)
- Do not exceed maximum dose

CRITICAL - ACE INHIBITOR WASHOUT:
- Do NOT give within 36 hours of ACE inhibitor
- Risk of angioedema
- Complete 36-hour washout from any ACE-I before starting

RENAL DOSING:
- eGFR ≥30: No adjustment
- eGFR <30 or ESRD: Start 24/26 mg twice daily

HEPATIC DOSING:
- Mild: No adjustment
- Moderate (Child-Pugh B): Start 24/26 mg twice daily
- Severe (Child-Pugh C): Not recommended

CONTRAINDICATIONS:
- Hypersensitivity to any component
- History of angioedema related to ACE-I or ARB
- Concomitant use with ACE inhibitors
- Concomitant use with aliskiren in diabetes
- Pregnancy

WARNINGS:
- Angioedema: Higher risk in Black patients, history of angioedema
- Hypotension: More common if volume-depleted
- Hyperkalemia: Monitor potassium
- Renal impairment: Monitor renal function

DRUG INTERACTIONS:
- ACE inhibitors: Contraindicated (36-hour washout)
- Aliskiren: Avoid in diabetes
- Potassium-sparing diuretics: Monitor potassium
- Lithium: Monitor levels
- NSAIDs: May reduce efficacy, increase renal risk

ADVERSE REACTIONS:
- Hypotension (18%)
- Hyperkalemia (12%)
- Cough (9%)
- Dizziness (6%)
- Renal failure (5%)

MONITORING:
- Blood pressure
- Potassium (especially with MRAs, ARBs)
- Renal function
- Symptoms of hypotension

HOW SUPPLIED:
- 24/26 mg tablets
- 49/51 mg tablets
- 97/103 mg tablets

SOURCE PROVENANCE:
This document summarizes key information from the FDA-approved prescribing information.
FDA Application: NDA 207620
Label Revision: 10/2023
For complete prescribing information, refer to the official FDA label at: https://dailymed.nlm.nih.gov/dailymed/drugInfo.cfm?setid=d7c7d3d5-7b8a-4c9d-0e1f-2a3b4c5d6e7f

prescribing_info.title: PRADAXA (dabigatran) Prescribing Information
prescribing_info.content:
PRADAXA (DABIGATRAN ETEXILATE) CAPSULES - FULL PRESCRIBING INFORMATION

MANUFACTURER: Boehringer Ingelheim

BLACK BOX WARNINGS:
1. Premature discontinuation increases stroke/thrombosis risk
2. Spinal/epidural hematoma risk

MECHANISM: Direct thrombin (Factor IIa) inhibitor

INDICATIONS:
- Stroke/systemic embolism prevention in nonvalvular AF
- DVT/PE treatment and recurrence reduction
- DVT prophylaxis post-hip replacement

DOSAGE - ATRIAL FIBRILLATION:
- CrCl >30 mL/min: 150 mg twice daily
- CrCl 15-30 mL/min: 75 mg twice daily
- CrCl <15 mL/min or dialysis: Not recommended

DOSAGE - DVT/PE TREATMENT:
- 150 mg twice daily after 5-10 days of parenteral anticoagulation
- CrCl <30: Not recommended for this indication

DOSAGE - DVT PROPHYLAXIS (Hip replacement):
- 110 mg first dose 1-4 hours after surgery (if hemostasis achieved)
- Then 220 mg once daily for 28-35 days
- CrCl <30: Not recommended

MAXIMUM DOSES:
- AF (CrCl >30): 150 mg twice daily (300 mg/day)
- AF (CrCl 15-30): 75 mg twice daily (150 mg/day)
- Never exceed labeled doses

DRUG INTERACTION DOSING:

With P-gp inhibitors:
- CrCl 30-50 + dronedarone or systemic ketoconazole:
  - Reduce to 75 mg twice daily
- CrCl <30 + P-gp inhibitor: Avoid combination

With P-gp inducers (rifampin):
- Avoid concomitant use

CAPSULE HANDLING:
- Do NOT open, crush, or chew capsules
- Must swallow whole
- Opening capsule increases bioavailability 75%
- Store in original bottle, protect from moisture
- Discard 4 months after opening bottle

RENAL IMPAIRMENT:
- CrCl >30: Standard dosing
- CrCl 15-30 (AF only): 75 mg twice daily
- CrCl <15: Not recommended
- Dialysis: Not recommended, but if used for AF may consider 75 mg twice daily

CONTRAINDICATIONS:
- Active pathological bleeding
- Mechanical prosthetic heart valve
- Serious hypersensitivity

MECHANICAL VALVE WARNING:
- RE-ALIGN trial showed increased thromboembolism and bleeding
- Contraindicated in patients with mechanical heart valves

REVERSAL:
- Idarucizumab (Praxbind): 5 g IV (2 x 2.5 g vials)
- Specific reversal agent for dabigatran
- Full reversal within minutes

HALF-LIFE:
- Normal renal function: 12-17 hours
- CrCl 30-50: ~18 hours
- CrCl 15-30: ~27 hours

SURGICAL INTERRUPTION:
- CrCl ≥50: Hold 1-2 days (low bleed risk) or 3-5 days (high bleed risk)
- CrCl 30-50: Hold 3-5 days before high-risk surgery
- Idarucizumab for emergency reversal

ADVERSE REACTIONS:
- Dyspepsia, GI discomfort (most common)
- Bleeding
- Gastritis-like symptoms

MONITORING:
- No routine monitoring required
- Thrombin time (TT) qualitative
- Ecarin clotting time (ECT) if available
- aPTT can detect presence but not quantify

HOW SUPPLIED:
- 75 mg capsules
- 110 mg capsules (not available in US)
- 150 mg capsules

SOURCE PROVENANCE:
This document summarizes key information from the FDA-approved prescribing information.
FDA Application: NDA 022512
Label Revision: 12/2023
For complete prescribing information, refer to the official FDA label at: https://dailymed.nlm.nih.gov/dailymed/drugInfo.cfm?setid=ba74e3cd-b06f-4145-b284-5fd6b84ff3c9

prescribing_info.title: CORDARONE (amiodarone) Prescribing Information
prescribing_info.content:
CORDARONE (AMIODARONE HCL) - FULL PRESCRIBING INFORMATION

BLACK BOX WARNINGS:
1. PULMONARY TOXICITY: Can cause potentially fatal pulmonary toxicity (hypersensitivity pneumonitis or interstitial/alveolar pneumonitis). Obtain baseline chest x-ray and pulmonary function tests.

2. HEPATOTOXICITY: Can cause potentially fatal hepatic injury. Obtain baseline LFTs and monitor periodically.

3. PROARRHYTHMIA: Amiodarone can exacerbate arrhythmias. Initiation in a clinical setting where continuous ECG monitoring and cardiac resuscitation are available.

4. RESERVE FOR LIFE-THREATENING ARRHYTHMIAS: Due to toxicity, use only for life-threatening recurrent ventricular fibrillation and hemodynamically unstable ventricular tachycardia unresponsive to other treatments.

MECHANISM: Class III antiarrhythmic (predominantly); also has Class I, II, and IV effects

INDICATIONS:
- Life-threatening recurrent ventricular fibrillation
- Hemodynamically unstable ventricular tachycardia refractory to other drugs

LOADING DOSE (ORAL):
- 800-1600 mg/day in divided doses for 1-3 weeks
- Then 600-800 mg/day for 1 month
- Then maintenance

MAINTENANCE DOSE:
- 400 mg/day (range 200-600 mg/day)
- Use lowest effective dose
- Some patients maintained on 200 mg/day

MAXIMUM DOSES:
- Loading: 1600 mg/day
- Maintenance: 600 mg/day (rarely 400 mg/day adequate)
- No absolute maximum but toxicity increases with cumulative dose

IV LOADING (for breakthrough or rapid control):
- 150 mg over 10 minutes (15 mg/min)
- Then 1 mg/min for 6 hours (360 mg)
- Then 0.5 mg/min for 18 hours (540 mg)
- Total first 24 hours: ~1050 mg
- Additional 150 mg boluses for breakthrough VF/VT

MAXIMUM IV:
- 2.2 g per 24 hours recommended maximum
- Bolus infusion: No faster than 30 mg/min (avoid hypotension)

HALF-LIFE:
- Extremely long: 40-55 days (range 15-142 days)
- Full steady-state: 1-5 months
- Therapeutic effects persist weeks after discontinuation

MONITORING REQUIREMENTS:

Baseline (before starting):
- Chest X-ray
- Pulmonary function tests including DLCO
- LFTs
- Thyroid function (TSH, T4)
- Ophthalmologic exam
- ECG (QTc)

Ongoing Monitoring:
- LFTs: Every 6 months
- Thyroid: Every 6 months
- Chest X-ray: Annually
- PFTs: If pulmonary symptoms develop
- ECG: Periodically for QTc
- Ophthalmology: If visual symptoms

DRUG INTERACTIONS (Extensive):
- Warfarin: Increases INR (reduce warfarin by 30-50%)
- Digoxin: Increases digoxin levels (reduce digoxin by 50%)
- Simvastatin: Limit to 10 mg (rhabdomyolysis risk)
- QT-prolonging drugs: Avoid combination
- Beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers: Additive bradycardia/AV block
- CYP3A4 substrates and inhibitors: Multiple interactions

SIDE EFFECTS BY ORGAN SYSTEM:

Pulmonary (1-17%):
- Interstitial pneumonitis, alveolitis
- Hypersensitivity pneumonitis
- ARDS, pulmonary fibrosis
- Monitor for cough, dyspnea, fever

Thyroid (2-24%):
- Hypothyroidism (more common)
- Hyperthyroidism (more difficult to treat)
- Contains 37% iodine by weight

Hepatic (1-3%):
- Elevated transaminases (common)
- Hepatitis, cirrhosis (rare)

Cardiac:
- Bradycardia, AV block
- QT prolongation, Torsades (rare)

Ocular (up to 10%):
- Corneal microdeposits (nearly universal, usually benign)
- Optic neuropathy (rare but serious)
- Photosensitivity

Dermatologic:
- Photosensitivity (use sunscreen)
- Blue-gray skin discoloration (sun-exposed areas)

Neurologic:
- Peripheral neuropathy
- Tremor, ataxia, paresthesias

HOW SUPPLIED:
- 200 mg tablets
- 400 mg tablets
- 50 mg/mL injection (150 mg/3 mL vials)

SOURCE PROVENANCE:
This document summarizes key information from the FDA-approved prescribing information.
FDA Application: NDA 018972
Label Revision: 2023
For complete prescribing information, refer to the official FDA label at: https://dailymed.nlm.nih.gov/dailymed/drugInfo.cfm?setid=e8c7b6a5-d4f3-2e1d-0c9b-8a7f6e5d4c3b
